As long as Uzziah sought the Lord and paid attention to religion, God made him prosper. Only those prosper whom God makes to prosper; for prosperity is his gift. Many have said that as long as they sought the Lord and stayed close to their duty, they prospered; but when they abandoned God, everything went wrong. God never continues to bless the lazy or withholds his blessing from the diligent. He will never allow anyone to seek his face in vain. Uzziah's name was famous throughout all the neighboring countries. A name with God and good people truly makes one honorable. He did not delight in war, nor indulge himself in sports, but delighted in farming.

The transgression of the kings before Uzziah was forsaking the temple of the Lord and burning incense upon idolatrous altars. But his transgression was going into the holy place and attempting to burn incense upon the altar of God. See how difficult it is to avoid one extreme and not run into another. Pride of heart was at the bottom of his sin; a desire that ruins many. Instead of lifting up the name of God in gratitude for him who had done so much for him, his heart was lifted up to his hurt. Men’s pretending to forbidden knowledge and seeking things too high for them are owing to pride of heart. The incense of our prayers must be, by faith, put into the hands of our Lord Jesus, the great High Priest of our profession, else we cannot expect it to be accepted by God. Though Uzziah argued with the priests, he would not argue with his Maker. But he was punished for his transgression; he remained a leper until his death, shut out from society. The punishment matched the sin as face to face in a mirror. Pride was at the bottom of his transgression, and thus God humbled him and brought dishonor upon him. Those who desire forbidden honors forfeit allowed ones. Adam, by reaching for the tree of knowledge which he might not eat of, deprived himself of the tree of life which he might have eaten of. Let all who read say, The Lord is righteous. And when the Lord sees good to set aside prosperous and useful men as broken vessels, if he raises up others to fill their places, they may rejoice to renounce all worldly concerns and spend their remaining days in preparation for death.
